quiz_questions.question_text: What are the three main types of scientific investigations?

options.text: Controlled experiments, observational studies, and models or simulations
Hypotheses, conclusions, and data tables only used in laboratories
Qualitative studies, quantitative studies, and theoretical calculations
Indoor experiments, outdoor experiments, and computer-based experiments

quiz_questions.explanation: The three main types of scientific investigations are controlled experiments (testing variables), observational studies (watching without manipulating), and models/simulations (representing real systems). Each serves different purposes.

quiz_questions.question_text: In which type of investigation does the scientist deliberately change a variable?

options.text: An observational study
A controlled experiment
A descriptive study only
A natural observation only

quiz_questions.explanation: In a controlled experiment, the scientist deliberately changes (manipulates) the independent variable while keeping all other variables constant. This allows cause-and-effect relationships to be determined.

quiz_questions.question_text: What type of investigation involves watching and recording data without changing anything?

options.text: A controlled experiment with a control group
An observational study that records natural events
A simulation using computer software to model
A laboratory experiment with multiple test groups

quiz_questions.explanation: An observational study involves collecting data by watching and recording events as they naturally occur, without manipulating any variables. Astronomers observing stars and ecologists counting animals are examples.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which type of investigation can establish cause-and-effect relationships?

options.text: Only observational studies done in natural settings
Only surveys conducted with large groups of people
Controlled experiments with manipulated variables
Descriptive studies using only qualitative data noted

quiz_questions.explanation: Only controlled experiments can establish cause-and-effect relationships because they isolate one variable while controlling all others. Observational studies can show correlations but cannot prove causation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist counts the number of bird species in a forest each month for a year. What type of investigation is this?

options.text: A controlled experiment
A field observation study
A computer simulation run
A laboratory experiment

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a field observation because the scientist is collecting data in a natural setting without manipulating any variables. She is simply recording what she observes about bird populations over time.

quiz_questions.question_text: When would a scientist use a model or simulation instead of a real experiment?

options.text: When the real event is too dangerous, expensive, large, or slow to test directly
When the scientist wants to guarantee the exact results they are expecting
When the scientist does not want to collect any real data from testing
When the experiment only requires simple observations to be made in the field

quiz_questions.explanation: Scientists use models or simulations when direct experimentation is impractical. For example, studying the formation of galaxies, predicting weather, or testing car crash safety requires simulations because real tests would be impossible, dangerous, or too expensive.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which investigation is best for studying how a volcanic eruption affects nearby ecosystems?

options.text: A controlled experiment in a school laboratory
An observational study of the ecosystem before and after the eruption
A survey asking people what they think about volcanic eruptions
A computer simulation that only uses hypothetical volcano data

quiz_questions.explanation: Since you cannot control a volcanic eruption, an observational study comparing the ecosystem before and after the event is the best approach. Scientists can document changes to wildlife, vegetation, and soil.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student tests whether salt concentration affects how fast ice melts. What type of investigation is this?

options.text: A natural observation
A controlled experiment
A descriptive study only
A computer simulation

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a controlled experiment because the student deliberately manipulates a variable (salt concentration) while controlling other factors, and measures the result (melting speed).

quiz_questions.question_text: A weather scientist uses computer programs to predict hurricane paths. What type of investigation is this?

options.text: A controlled laboratory experiment
A field observation of weather patterns
A model or simulation based on data
A survey of people affected by storms

quiz_questions.explanation: Using computer programs to predict hurricane paths is a simulation. The scientist inputs real data into mathematical models that simulate atmospheric conditions to forecast where the storm will go.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a key limitation of observational studies compared to controlled experiments?

options.text: They cannot establish cause-and-effect relationships between variables
They always produce less accurate data than experiments in every case
They are never used by professional scientists in any real research
They require more money and equipment than controlled experiments do

quiz_questions.explanation: Observational studies can identify correlations (things that happen together) but cannot prove that one variable causes another because variables are not controlled. Only controlled experiments can establish causation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a controlled experiment?

options.text: An investigation where one variable is changed while all others are kept constant
An investigation where the scientist only observes without changing any variables
An investigation that uses computer software to simulate a natural process
An investigation where all variables are changed at the same time for testing

quiz_questions.explanation: A controlled experiment is an investigation where the scientist deliberately changes one variable (independent) while keeping all others constant, and measures the effect on another variable (dependent).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an observational study?

options.text: An investigation where data is collected by watching events without manipulating variables
An investigation where the scientist changes one variable and measures the effect
An investigation conducted only in a laboratory with specialized equipment on hand
An investigation where the scientist creates a model to represent a real process

quiz_questions.explanation: An observational study involves watching, measuring, and recording data about events as they naturally occur without intervening or changing any variables. Field studies and surveys are common examples.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'field study' in science?

options.text: Research conducted in a natural environment rather than in a laboratory
Research conducted only in agricultural fields on farms and ranches
Research that involves studying only open grassy areas and meadows
Research where the scientist controls every variable in a closed space

quiz_questions.explanation: A field study is research conducted in the natural environment where events occur, such as a forest, ocean, or desert. Scientists collect data in real-world conditions rather than in a controlled laboratory.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'simulation' in scientific research?

options.text: A computer-based or physical imitation of a real process used for studying it
An experiment where all variables are kept exactly the same throughout testing
A natural observation recorded without any interference by the scientist
A measurement taken with an extremely precise instrument in a laboratory

quiz_questions.explanation: A simulation is a model that imitates a real-world process, often using computers. Simulations allow scientists to study systems that are too large, too fast, too slow, or too dangerous to study directly.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'descriptive investigation' mean?

options.text: An investigation that describes and records observations without testing a hypothesis
An investigation that uses only numbers and calculations with no word descriptions
An investigation that tests multiple hypotheses at the same time in one experiment
An investigation that provides a written description of the laboratory equipment

quiz_questions.explanation: A descriptive investigation involves observing and describing a subject or phenomenon without testing a specific hypothesis. Classifying rocks, cataloging species, or mapping terrain are examples.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'comparative investigation' mean?

options.text: An investigation that compares two or more groups or conditions without manipulation
An investigation where the scientist deliberately changes one variable being tested
An investigation that uses only one group of subjects with no comparison made
An investigation that compares the results from two completely different experiments

quiz_questions.explanation: A comparative investigation compares two or more groups, objects, or conditions to find similarities and differences. For example, comparing the soil in different locations or the behavior of different animal species.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'experimental investigation' mean?

options.text: An investigation that uses manipulation of variables to test cause and effect
Any scientific study regardless of whether variables are changed or controlled
An investigation that only involves observing events in the natural environment
An investigation that relies solely on predictions made before collecting data

quiz_questions.explanation: An experimental investigation involves deliberately manipulating one or more variables to observe the effect on an outcome. It is the most rigorous type of investigation for determining cause-and-effect relationships.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'correlation' mean in observational studies?

options.text: A relationship where two variables change together but one may not cause the other
A proven cause-and-effect link between two variables that has been confirmed
A situation where two variables are completely unrelated to each other in all ways
A mathematical error found during the analysis of experimental data in a lab

quiz_questions.explanation: Correlation means two variables change together in a pattern. However, correlation does not prove causation — the variables may both be influenced by a third factor rather than one causing the other.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'data collection' mean in an investigation?

options.text: The process of gathering information through measurements, observations, or surveys
The process of forming a hypothesis before any experiment has been performed
The process of writing a conclusion after all experimental analysis is complete
The process of selecting which equipment to use for a planned experiment

quiz_questions.explanation: Data collection is the systematic process of gathering information relevant to your investigation, whether through direct measurement, observation, surveys, or instrument readings.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'survey' as a type of investigation?

options.text: A method of gathering data by asking questions to a group of people
A controlled experiment conducted in a laboratory setting with equipment
A computer program that simulates natural processes using data inputs
A measurement taken using a specific instrument such as a thermometer

quiz_questions.explanation: A survey is a data collection method that uses questionnaires or interviews to gather information from a group of people. Surveys are useful for studying opinions, behaviors, and trends in populations.

quiz_questions.question_text: A marine biologist wants to study how dolphins interact in their natural habitat. She spends months observing dolphins from a boat without disturbing them. What type of investigation is she conducting?

options.text: A controlled experiment manipulating dolphin behavior
A field observation study in the dolphins' natural habitat
A computer simulation of dolphin swimming patterns
A laboratory experiment testing dolphin intelligence levels

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a field observation study because the biologist is watching and recording dolphin behavior in their natural environment without manipulating any variables or interfering with their activities.

quiz_questions.question_text: An engineer wants to test whether a new bridge design can withstand strong winds. It would be too expensive and dangerous to build a full-size bridge just for testing. What type of investigation should she use?

options.text: A field observation of existing bridges during storms
A model or simulation to test the design before building
A survey asking people if they think the bridge is safe
A descriptive study documenting wind patterns in the area

quiz_questions.explanation: A model (physical scale model) or computer simulation allows the engineer to test the bridge design under various wind conditions without the cost and risk of building a full-size structure.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student wants to know if the brand of battery affects how long a flashlight shines. She buys three brands, puts each in the same flashlight, and times how long each lasts. What type of investigation is this?

options.text: An observational study of battery performance in nature
A controlled experiment comparing battery brands directly
A simulation of battery life using computer software
A descriptive study recording the appearance of batteries

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a controlled experiment because the student deliberately changes the independent variable (battery brand) while keeping other factors constant (same flashlight), and measures the dependent variable (time).

quiz_questions.question_text: A geologist studies rock layers in the Grand Canyon to understand Earth's history. She cannot manipulate the rock layers or change geological time. What type of investigation is most appropriate?

options.text: A controlled experiment changing the conditions of rock formation
An observational and descriptive study of existing rock formations
A survey asking visitors what they think the rocks look like
A laboratory experiment creating artificial rock layers from scratch

quiz_questions.explanation: Since the geologist cannot manipulate rock layers or geological processes, an observational/descriptive study is most appropriate. She observes, describes, and records the characteristics of existing formations.

quiz_questions.question_text: A climate scientist wants to predict how temperatures will change over the next 50 years. She cannot wait 50 years for real data. What type of investigation should she use?

options.text: A controlled experiment in a laboratory over a 50-year period
A computer simulation using current data to model future trends
A field observation collecting temperature data for 50 more years
A survey asking people what temperature they expect it to be

quiz_questions.explanation: A computer simulation can model future climate conditions based on current data and trends. Since waiting 50 years is impractical, simulations allow scientists to explore possible outcomes much faster.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student reads that people who exercise more tend to have lower blood pressure. She wants to prove exercise causes lower blood pressure. Can she conclude this from the study she read?

options.text: Yes, because the study shows a clear cause-and-effect between the two factors
No, because the study only shows correlation and would need a controlled experiment
Yes, because all studies that show patterns automatically prove cause and effect
No, because blood pressure cannot be affected by any lifestyle choices at all

quiz_questions.explanation: The study shows a correlation (exercise and lower blood pressure occur together) but not causation. Other factors could explain the relationship. A controlled experiment would be needed to establish cause and effect.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronomer wants to study how stars form. She cannot create a star in a lab. What should she do?

options.text: Give up because star formation cannot be studied without a controlled experiment
Use observations of existing stars and computer simulations to study the process
Conduct a survey asking other scientists what they think about star formation
Only use data from textbooks and never conduct any new research on stars

quiz_questions.explanation: The astronomer can observe stars at different stages of formation using telescopes and use computer simulations to model the process. Many scientific phenomena are studied through combinations of observation and modeling.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student wants to compare the soil pH in three different parks. She collects soil samples and tests them but does not change any conditions. What type of investigation is she doing?

options.text: A controlled experiment with an independent variable changed
A comparative investigation looking at differences between sites
A computer simulation modeling soil chemistry processes in parks
An experimental investigation that will prove what causes pH levels

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a comparative investigation because she is comparing soil pH across three locations without manipulating any variables. She is looking for differences between the parks, not testing cause and effect.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher gives students a choice: study how temperature affects magnet strength (experiment) or study what types of rocks are found near school (observation). A student picks the observation study. What is one advantage of this choice?

options.text: It can prove that certain conditions cause specific types of rocks to form
It allows the student to study rocks in their real-world, natural environment
It is the only type of investigation that produces reliable scientific data
It requires no planning, preparation, or data recording from the student

quiz_questions.explanation: An observational study allows the student to examine rocks in their natural setting, which provides real-world context. However, she will not be able to determine cause-and-effect relationships from observations alone.

quiz_questions.question_text: NASA uses computer models to simulate how spacecraft will perform during launch. Why is a simulation better than a real test for the initial design phase?

options.text: Simulations always give perfectly accurate results that match real launches exactly
Simulations are cheaper and safer, allowing engineers to test many designs quickly
Real rocket launches cannot be observed or measured by any scientific instruments
Simulations require no data at all and are based entirely on random guesses only

quiz_questions.explanation: Computer simulations allow engineers to test hundreds of design variations quickly and safely without the enormous cost and risk of building and launching real rockets. Once a design passes simulation testing, real tests follow.
